attributes.stem:
A two-year-old boy presents to the General Practice clinic with his parent due to multiple changing skin lesions on his face and neck. His parent reports he develops severe sunburn even with minimal sun exposure. He did not have any similar issues before the age of 6 months. He has a history of mild developmental delay but no other medical conditions and takes no regular medications. His parent mentions that his older sister had similar symptoms and died from metastatic melanoma at age eight. His parents migrated from Japan last year.

On examination, he appears well with vital signs within normal limits. There are multiple hyperpigmented macules, areas of skin atrophy and telangiectasia. His eyes appear irritated and photophobic with sunglasses on inside the room.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Urgent dermatology referral with complete skin examination
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with classic features of xeroderma pigmentosum (XP) or DeSanctis-Cacchione syndrome, a rare autosomal recessive condition characterised by extreme sensitivity to ultraviolet radiation, early development of skin cancers, and neurological manifestations. The early-onset skin changes with initial unaffected skin at birth, and sibling death from melanoma strongly support this diagnosis. It is slightly more common in Japan though overal a rare disease and it has been diagnosed on all continents. There are multiple gene abnormalities causing XP and the severity is variable resulting in mild to severe disease and prognosis.

Urgent dermatology referral with complete skin examination is essential as patients with XP have a more than 10,000-fold increased risk of developing skin cancers, particularly in sun-exposed areas. Early detection and treatment of skin cancers is crucial for survival.

Annual skin examination would be inadequate given the aggressive nature of this condition. Patients with XP require frequent (every 3-6 months) complete skin examinations by a dermatologist to monitor for malignant changes.

Genetic counselling only would be insufficient as the immediate concern is the detection and management of potential skin cancers. While genetic counselling is important, it should be part of a comprehensive management plan rather than the primary intervention.

Topical corticosteroids would not address the underlying condition or prevent the development of skin cancers. They might provide temporary symptomatic relief for inflammation but would not be appropriate as primary management.

Wet dressings would be inadequate as they would only provide temporary symptomatic relief and would not address the underlying condition or prevent progression of skin changes and potential malignancy.

Management of XP requires a multidisciplinary approach including dermatologists, ophthalmologists, neurologists, and genetic counsellors. Prevention through strict UV protection (including protective clothing, sunscreen, and UV-protective windows) is essential from birth. Regular monitoring for skin cancers, eye complications, and neurological manifestations is crucial for optimal outcomes.
learningPoints: Xeroderma pigmentosum is an autosomal recessive condition causing extreme sensitivity to ultraviolet radiation.
Early onset of multiple skin cancers in childhood is characteristic of xeroderma pigmentosum.
Management focuses on strict sun protection from birth and regular skin surveillance.
references: Leung AK, Barankin B, Lam JM, Leong KF, Hon KL. Xeroderma pigmentosum: an updated review. Drugs Context. 2022.  Available at: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9045481/
Ngan V, Elghblawi E, Libya T, Oakley A.  Xeroderma pigmentosum.  DermNet NZ. 2017.  Available at: https://dermnetnz.org/topics/xeroderma-pigmentosum
Lehmann A.R., McGibbon D,  Stefanini M. Xeroderma pigmentosum. Orphanet Journal of Rare Diseases. 2011.  Available at: https://ojrd.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/1750-1172-6-70
